sentences.text: Well, it was anti-Semitism.
But, you know, as a child of four or five, how can you understand anti-Semitism?
When I asked my mother why the children wouldn't play with me, she said, you'll understand this later.
And this was one of the phrases that my mother used quite a bit.
And I hated this phrase as a child, you will understand it later.
Because, in fact, it's very difficult to understand.
So I was talking about my mother's benevolence.
The other benevolence was that Jewish people from Poland would come through our town.
Our town is in northeastern Moravia, so there were these expeditions to go and collect money for the Yeshivat in Poland.
And they were all people in kaftans.
They were all very colorful, you know, bearded, pious men with hats.
Many had distinct red hair.
There was even a group that used to come on bicycles, which my father used to call [GERMAN], which means the motorized ones.
And my father used to say that if-- if in a few days no beggars or no Jewish men would come asking for alms, my mother would go out of town looking for them, you know, like where are they?
But the conversations were always in Yiddish between my mother and these men.
They were always men.
And I-- you know, lots of people are put off by the kaftan and the-- and the garb and the behavior of [YIDDISH].
I never had that feeling, because my mother was so at one with these people, so I found them colorful.
And some of them would stay.
I mean, these people were so religious that even in our kosher house-- which was strictly kosher, and they could see the way that my mother spoke that it was kosher.
But even in our kosher house, some of these men would only eat a hard-boiled egg.
They would eat a piece of bread.
No.
Well, I do remember one, but I don't, I don't know his name.
I, I just remember that he stayed for the evening.
And he used to read a blatt Gemara with my father.
Gemara is the Talmud, and my father would take down a folio, and they would read together.
And I wouldn't understand any of this, because this was Aramaic, some often.
But they would then discuss it in Yiddish, and I wouldn't understand that either, because they were talking about matters which were well beyond a child's understanding.
I looked on, agog.
My mother would serve tea, Russian tea with lumps of sugar.
And I was always fascinated how this beard would open up, usually bad teeth, and these men would pop a lump of sugar in their mouth, and then start sipping.
And this one man that used to stay till the evening to talk with my father a blatt Gemara, he used to amuse me by throwing the sugar up in the air and catching it when it was coming down.
So I was always fascinated by these, these men, but I always listened.
Once I remember the motorized ones came, and they had a young boy with them.
And my mother took a fancy to this young boy.
She started talking to him, you know.
Oh, whenever my mother talked to these men, they were always relatives.
And if they weren't relatives, they were neighbors of relatives.
[LAUGHS]
My mother knew every town in Poland.
She knew the geography, and she would sort of-- they would say where they are from or which yeshiva they are from.
And she would say, do you know so-and-so?
And there would be a whole long conversation about who they know, and do you know such and such?
And it would be very lively.
And that's how I learned Yiddish.
So I sort of was trilingual, I suppose.
I never learned to read Yiddish until after the war, a few years back.
What memories do you have of regular schooling at that time?
Well, I first went to a kindergarten when I was five.
And I don't actually remember the fights that I had there.
You know, I, I was not, not one to complain if I got into a scrap.
Well, I, I used to be beaten up in this kindergarten.
And the kindergarten was a German kindergarten.
It was just down the road from us.
The, the buildings are still there, but it's no-- yes, and it's still a kindergarten.
They've built a swimming pool opposite this kindergarten, now.
But this was a German kindergarten, and it was all modern.
We each had our own cupboard, you know.
And it was all colored in blue and red.
And there were sand pits for play.
And it was all lovely.
But there were kids there which were, who were 10, 11, 12 years old.
And what happened-- now, I explain it, why these kids were there.
They were all retarded.
They were retarded German children who couldn't make normal school, so they stayed in the kindergarten.
You know, in the kindergarten you had to pay.
But you know, a retarded 10-year-old, if you are five, can beat you up.
[LAUGHS] But these German children had in them the fact that I was a Jewish boy.
And I had to fight.
So I came home with grazed knees and grazed elbows every day.
And the grazes always opened up, you know, because I fought.
And I mean, I did the best I could and-- well, when the fight got really big, the teachers broke it up.
So my mother went to complain.
And it went on for two or three months, through the winter.
And then I remember the springtime was lovely, because-- this was from 8:00 in the morning until 11:00, the kindergarten.
We used to come home at 11:00.
I'd be ravenously hungry, and my mother would put up a table and chair in the garden and give me something nice to eat.
I was a bad eater, but bread I ate, and butter.
And I loved cottage cheese, and I loved strawberries.
And sour milk, sour milk I would drink.
I wouldn't drink milk.
I wouldn't eat soup, except potato soup.
My mother had lots of troubles with me with eating.
And I was small for my age, so she was very worried that-- that I was being beaten up every day.
And in fact, I didn't last until the end of the thing, because my mother got called to the school, and I was accused of depositing my feces in my cupboard.
And my mother said I wasn't that kind of a boy.
And I wasn't that kind of a boy.
I just wasn't.
And in fact, you know, one of these other kids did it, some sort of spite.
And over this row, my mother took me out of the school.
So that was about March, perhaps April.
And that must have been 1934, because I was five.
Oh, well, because it was a German town, my brothers were in similar fights.
So I was following their examples, you know, that we all had problems at school.
And we all had problems with the German population.
I had an inkling already, because this was 1934.

And I told you-- well, I didn't tell you on the interview, but we spoke about it before that in 1933, the German Nazi party immediately became dominant in the town, and the mayor of the town became a Nazi mayor.
And I mean, we were all sporty, and we skied.
And summer, we went swimming.
And we played football.
And the favorite sport was calisthenics in those days, gymnastics.
Well, we couldn't skate.
The skating rink was in the German part of town, and whenever any one of the Jewish youths from [NON-ENGLISH] went skating, he got beaten up.
So I knew that there were, there were these fights in the town.
And we were on the side of the Czechs, or the Czechs were on our side, and the Germans were against us.
So when I found it in, in kindergarten, I sort of-- to me, it was the natural course of events.
That's how we lived.
It wasn't a drama.
It wasn't even a problem.
I mean, I remember once on tricycle-- I was on a tricycle outside my house, and opposite us was a large school.
It was a trade school, so kids went there from the age of 10 to 14.
It wasn't a gymnasium.
And we used to lease it on Wednesday evening, or Wednesday afternoons, actually, to have to-- we used to lease the gym, [NON-ENGLISH], in this German school.
But by this time, the school was empty.
There were no kids there anymore.
And we used to have gym classes, you know, the Jewish Youth Movement.
When I went to school next year, it was 1935, and that was Czech school.
And that was absolutely lovely, because there was, there was no antipathy at all.
The whole thing was, was-- I, I really enjoyed school.
It was, it was marvelous.
I had a very sympathetic teacher, very sort of-- he was a wonderful man.
And he could see that I could read.
I don't know how I could read, but I could read.
And he would put me in the back of the class somewhere and give me books.
They were kids' books, you know, they weren't-- nothing-- [LAUGHS] it wasn't Kant or something.
But, but he could see that I was miles ahead of the kids.
But that's how elementary school started.
And elementary school, I mean, I never had to do any work, because whatever I was told I knew.
Yes, lots.
Well, all my brothers' books.
And they were German and Czech, and they were all his school, all their school books.
So I got hold of their school books, and I played with them and looked at them and-- I mean, we had an encyclopedia, which I used to, used to thumb through, looking at the pictures, mainly.
But I mean, some of the things, I even remember.
There were two things in the encyclopedia that caught my mind, and I remembered it till adulthood, these pages.
One was a picture of Moses.
And I mean, I always remember it.
It was the Church of Saint Vincoli in Rome.
And when I was an adult, I went to Rome, and I went to the church in Vincoli.
And you know, Vincoli means chains, I think.
And there's a statue of Moses by Michelangelo there.
And that statue sort of lived with me.
It was one thing that impressed me.
It looks a little like Rodin's Rodin probably took the idea from Michelangelo.
I don't know.
Perhaps or perhaps not.
But the other-- and the other one was the Laocoon.
And the Laocoon sort of took my fancy because of the story.
It's a Greek man with his sons who is fighting serpents.
And there are serpents-- it's a statue.
I don't think it's Michelangelo, but it's that sort of period.
I forget who it's by, but it's a famous statue.
And it's in the-- it's in the Vatican.
And again in adult life, I went to see that statue.
And it's a story of this man being punished by Hera, who sends the snakes to kill him and his sons.
And they fight these serpents, you see.
So this caught my imagination.
What a terrible death it must be to, to be, to be asphyxiated by serpents.
But I mean, you know, whilst this is a horror story, a child doesn't see it like that.
You just sort of-- it's a story.
It's a, it's, it's-- I think I had an interest in history ever since that sort of, that sort of thing.

Yes, we went to cheder, and it was quite ineffectual because the teachers were inadequate, and it was reform, and it was-- the only thing that's interesting is that when I was learning Hebrew after the war, in 1948 in Israel, I still remember the pronunciation, which is this German reform, you see, like the vav with a dot is 'ow' instead of 'oh,' which it is both in Ashkenazi and in Sephardi Hebrew.
And there are all kinds of differences in pronunciation.
You know, I do genealogy.
I was looking up somebody called Horowitz.
And Horowitz is written with a hey vav with a dot, which I read Horowitz.
And in Yiddish, everybody says Horowitz, and-- but you see it in German written text, and it's written Haurowitz, H-A-U, which is the German reform pronunciation of the Hebrew vav with a dot on top.
Well, there were, there were there are lots of memories.
The next really impressionable memory was when I could first go to Maccabi Hatzair camp.
That was when I was six.
I was pestering my parents, you know, that I should go to Maccabi camp in the summer.
Because in the summer, we were-- the house was full of men all the time and us two children.
All the others, they were like giants, these fellas-- my brothers.
When my brothers got angry with each other, and they fought-- I mean it was like the Laocoon, you know.
It was a-- [LAUGHS] the whole house shook.
And my mother would come in with the carpet beater, and she would separate them with this carpet beater.
And she would lay into them quite, quite, you know-- because they wouldn't desist, sometimes.
They were boxing and all kinds of things.
But I mean it was all-- that, you know, that sort of fight arises all the time.
But it was wondrous to me as a boy to see these men, sometimes, fighting.
And you take sides, you see.
You'd have favorite brothers and not-so-favorite brothers.
[LAUGHS]
So my, my first memory was, for example, going skiing when I was five.
And my brothers went skiing, so I followed.
There was a little girl in the town called [NON-ENGLISH].
They were completely sort of German reform, not even religious.
They never came to synagogue.
But my mother and this lady were sort of thinking that these two kids belong together.
And somehow, we-- it-- it was understood that they were thinking of us as a couple.
And I mean, we were children of five or something.
And the way that we fell apart with this Helen, Helen [NON-ENGLISH]-- she's alive.
She lives in, in Australia.

And she married a religious Jew, and she's become religious, and I'm completely [INAUDIBLE] [LAUGHS] so-- But the way that we broke up was when-- we broke up when we were five, so the match never, the match which my mother was planning never came to anything.
Because my mother wasn't sure that I would like skiing.
She thought that I'd get scared and be timid.
So she bought the skis, and she-- she bought the boots because the boots were good anyway for the winter.
And the binding was a sort of child's binding, which was like a sandal, so that you could take ordinary, high, good boots and ski.
But she wasn't going to buy the skiing trousers, because-- she was going to buy the skiing trousers only after she saw that I would, that I would ski.
And the skiing trial-- I always had hand-me-downs from, from my brothers.
And [NON-ENGLISH] skiing trousers were too large for me.
So she borrowed the skiing trousers of this girl Helen.
And it didn't have buttons, you see.
They were not boys', boys' trousers.
So I refused to wear them.
So she said, well, you can't go skiing, then, because you've got to have skiing trousers for the snow.
We used to have very deep snow in Novy Jicin, big, powdery drifts of snow.
So I wanted to go skiing so much that in the end, I relented and took girls' trousers.
And we got to the top of a hill, after some marching, and my brother [?
Mordche ?] said, look, you follow in my footsteps.
There was completely clear snow.
We went down a road, sort of winding.
And he says, if you go in my, in my marks, that will guide you so you don't have to steer.
Just go down on your haunches, and don't stand up.
Just stay on your haunches until you come to a stop at the bottom, because it's flat at the bottom.
And Mordche was one of these brothers that I-- that was one of my-- I liked him, you see.
So when Mordche said something, I did automatically exactly what he said.
He didn't have to tell me twice, and he didn't even try to impose himself.
He would just tell me, this is how you do it.
And I would do it.
And I came down without falling, and I loved it.

[LAUGHS] But the problem was that I was so small, and I got so tired going out of town and doing this run down, that they said, well, you're not up to going with us, because we're going another few miles.
And then we're going up and down the hill, so it'll be too hard for you.
So you sit here in the pub, and they'll give you a cup of tea, and-- and we'll come back for you.
There were three of them-- Shragga, Mordche, and Milush.
They were skiing together.
And me.
So I sort of stayed there, and I was happy enough to be left because I was tired.
And I was given a cup of tea.
That meant lemon tea.
And of course, half an hour later, I needed to go.
But I had girls' trousers, you see, so I was afraid to tell the publican that I had girls' trousers, that I needed to go to the toilet where I could sit down.
Because in Czechoslovakia, if a boy needed to go, you just went around the corner, and you just did it in nature.
And I couldn't do it in nature.
I had to go to the proper toilet.
And it wasn't like here where you go to the flush toilet.
So I sort of sat there in agony, what seemed, now, like hours, till they came.
But I was happy enough when they came, and I still didn't go to the toilet until we got home.
But because of the shame of wearing these girls' trousers, I refused to go and play with this girl.
And I had actually not seen her since.
[LAUGHS] I never-- I wrote her about this story.
She doesn't remember this.
And it's very amusing, but it's very, you know, it's sort of formative in, in my, in my own thing that I-- this was the first girlfriend that I threw over.
[LAUGHS]
The next main event was-- well, we went periodically to Poland.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The next main event was-- I was going to tell you-- going to this Maccabi Hatzair camp, because it was in Zilina in-- in Slovakia, which in those days was a day's journey by train.
And I was sent alone.
And there were other benyamin.
Benyamin means the youngest, you know, like Benjamin was the youngest tribe.
Six.
And the hut was in the mountains above Zilina.
Very long walk, very tiring climb.
You know, you had to carry a rucksack, and you had to carry your own.
Most of the other kids were older than me.
I was sort of the youngest and the smallest.
But I wasn't going to give up or anything, you know.
That's how I was taught.
You know, our family didn't, didn't-- so we got there, and I was exhausted.
But it was, it was a gorgeous place.
And I remember this sort of meadow.
In the middle is this wooden hut, one story, timber frame, you know, like a Canadian hut.
That kind of thing.
Large building, a log-- really, a glorified log cabin.
This was in Zilina, near Zilina, in the mountains near Zilina.
My brother Shragga was saying that we ought to claim it, because it belongs to the Youth Movement.
It belonged Maccabi Hatzair.
And I remember it particularly well, because a friend of mine, a friend of my brother Shragga-- Shragga was, by then, in the national leadership of Maccabi Hatzair.
He's the middle brother, if you remember.
And a friend of his, who was also in the national leadership, came to lead the camp.
And his name was Freddy Hirsch.
Freddy had also come to our home several times on inspections.
You know the gymnastics, Maccabi?
So he would inspect how we-- you know, whether the organization of the [NON-ENGLISH] was-- was going well.
My brother Mordche was in charge of [NON-ENGLISH], and my brother Shragga was already in the national leadership, and he was already at school in this textile school.
I think we'll stop the tape now.
